root_cause: The web service was configured for aggressive retries against a degraded pricing dependency (MAX_RETRIES=4, DEP_TIMEOUT_MS=5000), so each /order could hold a worker for up to ~25s. Pricing always failed after ~2s latency (FAIL_EVERY=1). With only 8 workers and concurrent load, the pool saturated: health checks returned 503 ("pool wedged") and users could not place orders despite idle CPU — a classic retry storm / thread-pool exhaustion, not a crash.
actions_taken: Recreated sreft-web with RETRY_STORM_DISABLED=1 (matching the healthy payments service: bounded retries, 400ms dep timeout, fail-fast) so workers are not held by slow failures. Recreated sreft-pricing with FAIL_EVERY=0 and reduced latency so the dependency returns success, allowing orders to complete. Verified /healthz=200 and concurrent /order=200.
postmortem: Blameless postmortem: A slow, always-failing pricing dependency combined with unbounded-style retries on web turned a partial dependency outage into a full web outage via worker pool exhaustion. CPU looked fine because workers were blocked on I/O waits, not compute. The payments service already demonstrated the safer pattern (fail fast, shed load). Follow-ups: (1) default all callers to short timeouts and capped retries with backoff/jitter; (2) separate liveness from dependency health so health checks do not require free workers blocked on deps; (3) add bulkheads/circuit breakers and load-shedding when the pool is near saturation; (4) alert on worker utilization and dependency latency/error rate before the pool wedges; (5) chaos-test retry policies against slow/erroring deps in staging.
